content: Student's t-test compares means of two groups: t = (x1_bar - x2_bar) / sqrt(s1²/n1 + s2²/n2). Use when n < 30 or population std unknown. Paired t-test for before/after measurements. Welch's t-test when variances are unequal (default in most software).

Chi-squared test for categorical data: chi² = sum((Observed - Expected)² / Expected). Tests independence between two categorical variables. Degrees of freedom = (rows-1)(cols-1). Requires expected count >= 5 in each cell; use Fisher's exact test otherwise.

ANOVA (Analysis of Variance): tests if 3+ group means differ significantly. F-statistic = between-group variance / within-group variance. If F > F_critical, at least one group differs. Follow up with Tukey's HSD for pairwise comparisons.

p-value is P(observing data this extreme | null hypothesis true). p < 0.05 is conventional threshold but arbitrary. Report effect size alongside p-value. With large samples, tiny meaningless effects can be 'significant'. Bayesian analysis avoids p-value problems.

Type I error (false positive): rejecting true null hypothesis. Rate = alpha (typically 0.05). Type II error (false negative): failing to reject false null. Rate = beta. Power = 1-beta. Target power >= 0.80. Bonferroni correction for multiple comparisons: alpha/k.

Confidence interval: CI = x_bar ± z * (s/sqrt(n)). 95% CI uses z=1.96. 99% CI uses z=2.576. Interpretation: if we repeated the experiment many times, 95% of intervals would contain the true parameter. NOT 'the parameter has 95% chance of being in this interval'.

Bootstrap confidence intervals: resample data with replacement B=10000 times, compute statistic each time, take 2.5th and 97.5th percentiles. Works for any statistic (median, ratio, correlation). No distributional assumptions needed.

Bayes theorem: P(H|D) = P(D|H) * P(H) / P(D). Posterior ∝ Likelihood × Prior. Conjugate priors simplify computation: Beta-Binomial (proportions), Normal-Normal (means), Gamma-Poisson (rates). Credible intervals give direct probability statements about parameters.

MCMC (Markov Chain Monte Carlo): samples from posterior distribution when analytical solution is intractable. Metropolis-Hastings proposes moves, accepts with probability min(1, posterior_ratio). NUTS (No U-Turn Sampler) auto-tunes, used in PyMC and Stan.

ARIMA(p,d,q): p = autoregressive order (past values), d = differencing order (stationarity), q = moving average order (past errors). Use ACF for q (cuts off at lag q) and PACF for p (cuts off at lag p). Test stationarity with ADF test (p < 0.05 = stationary).

Prophet (Meta): additive decomposition y(t) = trend + seasonality + holidays + error. Handles missing data, outliers, and multiple seasonalities automatically. Best for business time series with strong seasonal patterns. pip install prophet.

Exponential smoothing (Holt-Winters): Simple (level only), Double/Holt (level + trend), Triple/Holt-Winters (level + trend + seasonality). Smoothing parameters alpha (level), beta (trend), gamma (seasonality) control adaptation speed. Values 0.1-0.3 typical.

XGBoost hyperparameters: learning_rate=0.1 (lower = better but slower), max_depth=6 (deeper = more complex, overfit risk), n_estimators=100-1000 (more = better until overfitting), min_child_weight=1, subsample=0.8, colsample_bytree=0.8. Use early stopping.

Random Forest: ensemble of decision trees trained on bootstrap samples with random feature subsets. n_estimators=100-500 (more is better, diminishing returns after 500). max_features=sqrt(n_features) for classification, n_features/3 for regression.

SVM kernels: linear (linearly separable data), RBF (most common, works for non-linear), polynomial (specific non-linear patterns). RBF has C (regularization) and gamma (influence radius) parameters. Use GridSearchCV for tuning. Scale features first.

Ensemble methods: bagging (parallel, reduces variance — Random Forest), boosting (sequential, reduces bias — XGBoost/LightGBM), stacking (meta-learner on top of base learners). Stacking typically adds 1-3% accuracy over best single model.

F1 score = 2 * (Precision * Recall) / (Precision + Recall). Use for imbalanced classes where accuracy is misleading. Precision = TP/(TP+FP), Recall = TP/(TP+FN). F1 balances both. For multi-class: macro (equal weight) vs weighted (by support).

AUC-ROC: area under Receiver Operating Characteristic curve. Measures discrimination regardless of threshold. AUC=0.5 is random, 0.7-0.8 is acceptable, 0.8-0.9 is good, >0.9 is excellent. Use AUC-PR (Precision-Recall) for highly imbalanced datasets.

OLS regression assumptions: linearity (plot residuals vs fitted), normality of residuals (Q-Q plot, Shapiro-Wilk), homoscedasticity (constant variance, Breusch-Pagan test), independence (Durbin-Watson for autocorrelation), no multicollinearity (VIF < 10).

Ridge regression (L2): adds lambda * sum(beta²) penalty. Shrinks coefficients toward zero but never exactly zero. Handles multicollinearity well. Lasso (L1): adds lambda * sum(|beta|) penalty. Drives coefficients to exactly zero — performs feature selection.

ElasticNet combines L1 and L2: penalty = alpha * (l1_ratio * |beta| + (1-l1_ratio) * beta²). l1_ratio=1 is pure Lasso, l1_ratio=0 is pure Ridge. l1_ratio=0.5 is balanced. Use when features are correlated and you want both selection and grouping.

VIF (Variance Inflation Factor): VIF_j = 1/(1-R²_j) where R²_j is R-squared of regressing feature j on all other features. VIF > 5 = concerning, VIF > 10 = severe multicollinearity. Fix: remove one of correlated features, PCA, or use Ridge regression.

BPE (Byte-Pair Encoding) tokenization: iteratively merges most frequent byte pairs. GPT uses BPE with ~50K vocab. WordPiece (BERT) is similar but maximizes likelihood. SentencePiece handles any language without pre-tokenization. Average: 1 token ≈ 4 characters.

Named Entity Recognition (NER): identifies entities (person, org, location, date, money) in text. spaCy NER is fast for production. BERT-based NER (fine-tuned on CoNLL-2003) achieves F1 > 90%. Use for extracting structured data from unstructured text.

VADER sentiment (rule-based): fast, no training needed, works well on social media. Returns compound score [-1, 1]. FinBERT (learned): fine-tuned BERT for financial text, 85% accuracy on financial datasets. Use VADER for speed, FinBERT for financial accuracy.

StandardScaler: zero mean, unit variance. z = (x - mean) / std. Use for algorithms sensitive to scale (SVM, KNN, neural nets, PCA). MinMaxScaler: scales to [0,1]. x_scaled = (x - min) / (max - min). Use when you need bounded values. RobustScaler uses median/IQR for outlier resistance.

One-hot encoding: creates binary columns per category. Use for < 10 categories. Target encoding: replaces category with mean of target variable. Use for high cardinality (100+ categories). Add smoothing to prevent overfitting: encoded = (count * mean + global_mean * m) / (count + m).

PCA (Principal Component Analysis): find directions of maximum variance. Keep components explaining 95% of variance. n_components = min(n_samples, n_features). Always standardize features first. Use explained_variance_ratio_ to determine how many components to keep.

SHAP values: unified measure of feature importance from game theory. SHAP(feature) = average marginal contribution across all feature coalitions. TreeSHAP is fast for tree models. Provides both global importance and per-prediction explanations.

Mutual information for feature selection: MI(X,Y) = sum(p(x,y) * log(p(x,y) / (p(x)*p(y)))). Captures non-linear relationships (unlike correlation). sklearn.feature_selection.mutual_info_classif/regression. Higher MI = more informative feature.

Isolation Forest for anomaly detection: isolates anomalies by random partitioning. Anomalies require fewer splits to isolate. contamination parameter sets expected anomaly fraction (default 0.1). Good for high-dimensional data, no assumption on distribution.

Z-score anomaly detection: |z| > 3 flags outliers (assumes normality). IQR method: outlier if x < Q1 - 1.5*IQR or x > Q3 + 1.5*IQR. IQR is more robust to skewed distributions. For time series: use rolling z-score with window matching seasonality.

DBSCAN clustering: density-based, finds arbitrarily shaped clusters. Parameters: eps (neighborhood radius) and min_samples (minimum points for core). Automatically detects outliers (noise points). Use k-distance graph to find optimal eps.

Cross-validation strategies: k-fold (k=5 or 10, standard), stratified k-fold (preserves class ratios, use for imbalanced), time series split (never leak future data), leave-one-out (expensive, use for tiny datasets), group k-fold (prevent data leakage from grouped observations).

Missing value strategies: MCAR (missing completely at random → any imputation), MAR (missing at random → model-based imputation), MNAR (not at random → domain knowledge needed). Methods: mean/median (simple), KNN imputation (considers neighbors), MICE (iterative multivariate).

Autoencoder anomaly detection: train neural network to reconstruct normal data. Anomalies have high reconstruction error because the model hasn't learned their patterns. Threshold: mean + 3*std of reconstruction errors on validation set.

LightGBM vs XGBoost: LightGBM uses leaf-wise growth (faster, less memory, risk of overfitting on small data). XGBoost uses level-wise growth (more conservative). LightGBM 10-20x faster on large datasets. Both produce similar accuracy with proper tuning.

Temporal Fusion Transformer (TFT): attention-based architecture for multi-horizon time series forecasting. Variable selection networks auto-identify important features. Static covariate encoding handles non-time-varying features. State-of-the-art for financial forecasting.

Data leakage prevention: never use test data for any decision (feature selection, scaling parameters, hyperparameter tuning). For time series: always split chronologically. For grouped data: keep groups in same fold. Pipeline() in sklearn prevents feature leakage.

Correlation types: Pearson (linear relationship, -1 to 1), Spearman (monotonic relationship, rank-based), Kendall (concordance, more robust with small samples). Use Spearman for non-linear monotonic relationships. Correlation ≠ causation — always check for confounders.

A/B testing sample size: n = (z_alpha/2 + z_beta)² * (p1*(1-p1) + p2*(1-p2)) / (p1-p2)². For 5% significance, 80% power, detecting 2% conversion lift from 10% baseline: need ~3,800 per group. Use sequential testing to stop early if effect is clear.

Bayesian A/B testing: model conversion as Beta(successes+1, failures+1). Compute P(A > B) by sampling. Stop when P(A > B) > 0.95 or < 0.05. Advantages over frequentist: no fixed sample size needed, direct probability statements, can stop anytime.

Feature interaction terms: multiply features to capture combined effects. x1*x2 captures interaction that linear model misses. sklearn.preprocessing.PolynomialFeatures(interaction_only=True) generates all pairwise interactions. Use with Lasso to select important ones.

Calibration of probabilistic models: well-calibrated means predicted 70% should be correct 70% of the time. Check with reliability diagram (calibration curve). Fix with Platt scaling (logistic on scores) or isotonic regression. CalibratedClassifierCV in sklearn.

Dimensionality curse: as dimensions increase, distance metrics become meaningless (all points become equidistant). KNN degrades above ~20 dimensions. Reduce dimensions first with PCA, UMAP, or t-SNE for visualization (2-3D). UMAP preserves global structure better than t-SNE.

Class imbalance techniques: oversampling minority (SMOTE generates synthetic examples), undersampling majority (random or Tomek links), class weights in loss function (class_weight='balanced'), threshold adjustment. SMOTE + Random Forest is a strong baseline.

ETL pipeline design: Extract (batch from APIs/DBs, streaming from Kafka/webhooks), Transform (clean, validate, enrich, aggregate), Load (append to data warehouse/lake). Use Prefect or Dagster for orchestration. Idempotent transforms enable safe reruns.

Data quality checks: completeness (% non-null), uniqueness (no duplicates on key columns), consistency (cross-table validation), timeliness (data freshness), accuracy (spot-check against source). Great Expectations library automates these checks as test suites.

Survival analysis: Kaplan-Meier estimates survival function from censored data. Cox Proportional Hazards models hazard as function of covariates: h(t) = h0(t) * exp(beta*X). Use for churn prediction (time to churn), customer lifetime modeling.

Network analysis centrality measures: degree (connections count), betweenness (lies on shortest paths between others), closeness (average distance to all nodes), eigenvector (connected to important nodes). PageRank is a variant of eigenvector centrality with damping.
